summary: SKCanvasView in Blazor throws unhandled TypeError during Dispose() when SizeWatcherInterop.Stop() calls ResizeObserver.unobserve() with an undefined element reference, caused by the DOM element being removed before the component is disposed.

bugSignals.errorMessage: TypeError: Failed to execute 'unobserve' on 'ResizeObserver': parameter 1 is not of type 'Element'
bugSignals.stackTrace: Microsoft.JSInterop.JSException → JSInProcessObjectReference.Invoke → JSModuleInterop.Invoke → SizeWatcherInterop.Stop() → SizeWatcherInterop.OnDisposingModule() → JSModuleInterop.Dispose() → SKCanvasView.Dispose()

stepsToReproduce: Create a Blazor WASM application with multiple SKCanvasView components
Show and hide components containing SKCanvasView repeatedly
Observe unhandled exception in console from Dispose()
reproEvidence.environmentDetails: Blazor WASM, .NET 8.0, SkiaSharp 3.116.0, Visual Studio Windows

screenshots.description: Console error showing unhandled exception in Dispose

versionAnalysis.relevanceReason: The SizeWatcher.js unobserve function still lacks a null guard on the element lookup, and JSModuleInterop.Dispose() does not catch JS exceptions.

analysis.summary: The SizeWatcher.js unobserve() function calls ResizeObserver.unobserve(element) without checking if the element retrieved from the Map is defined. When Blazor removes the DOM element before disposing the component, the Map lookup returns undefined, causing the TypeError. Additionally, the C# Dispose() chain has no exception handling for JS interop failures during cleanup.
analysis.rationale: This is a clear bug: Dispose() should never throw unhandled exceptions. The JS code lacks a null guard, and the C# disposal chain doesn't catch JSException. The reporter's secondary point about missing Dispose(bool) pattern is valid but is an enhancement request, not the root cause. Similar to #2441 (NullReferenceException in Blazor Dispose, fixed in 2.88.4) but a different code path. #3065 addressed a related memory leak where SizeWatcher never released canvas views.

keySignals.text: TypeError: Failed to execute 'unobserve' on 'ResizeObserver': parameter 1 is not of type 'Element'
keySignals.interpretation: ResizeObserver.unobserve() received undefined instead of an Element — the element was removed from the DOM before disposal.

keySignals.text: When show and hide multiple SKCanvasView, an exception observed from Dispose()
keySignals.interpretation: Rapid show/hide creates a race between DOM element removal and component disposal.

keySignals.text: the Dispose() in SKCanvasView() does not follow proper .net Dispose pattern
keySignals.interpretation: Secondary enhancement request — lack of virtual Dispose(bool) prevents derived classes from suppressing the exception.

codeInvestigation.finding: unobserve() retrieves element via SizeWatcher.elements.get(elementId) at line 20, but line 22 calls SizeWatcher.observer.unobserve(element) without checking if element is defined. When the element was already removed or never registered, this passes undefined to ResizeObserver.unobserve(), causing the TypeError.
Dispose() calls OnDisposingModule() then Module.Dispose() with no try-catch. Any JSException from the JS interop propagates as an unhandled exception during disposal.
OnDisposingModule() directly calls Stop() which invokes the JS Unobserve function. No exception handling wraps this call.

Dispose() calls sizeWatcher?.Dispose(), interop?.Dispose(), then FreeBitmap() sequentially. If sizeWatcher throws, interop and FreeBitmap are skipped — causing a GCHandle leak from the pinned pixel buffer.

nextQuestions: Does the same issue affect SKGLView.razor.cs in Blazor?
Is the element removal timing a Blazor framework behavior or specific to conditional rendering patterns?
Would switching to IAsyncDisposable be more appropriate for Blazor components with JS interop?

resolution.hypothesis: When Blazor conditionally hides a component, the DOM element is removed before Dispose() runs. The SizeWatcher.elements Map may still have the elementId but the actual DOM element reference becomes stale, causing ResizeObserver.unobserve() to fail.

proposals.title: Add null guard in SizeWatcher.js unobserve
proposals.description: Add an element existence check before calling ResizeObserver.unobserve() in the JavaScript code. This prevents the TypeError at its source.

proposals.title: Add try-catch in JSModuleInterop.Dispose and SKCanvasView.Dispose
proposals.description: Defense-in-depth: wrap each disposal step in its own try-catch to ensure subsequent cleanup (including FreeBitmap/GCHandle) always runs even if JS interop fails.

resolution.recommendedProposal: Add null guard in SizeWatcher.js unobserve
resolution.recommendedReason: Fixes the root cause at the JS level with a trivial one-line change. Combined with the C# try-catch defense-in-depth, this ensures both the immediate error and any future JS interop failures during disposal are handled gracefully.

actionability.reason: Root cause is clear from code analysis but needs reproduction to verify the fix and check if SKGLView has the same issue.

actions.description: Apply bug, Blazor views, and WASM labels
Acknowledge bug, explain root cause, note no safe user-side workaround
actions.comment:
Thanks for the detailed stack trace — this clearly points to the issue.

The root cause is in `SizeWatcher.js`: the `unobserve()` function doesn't check whether the element reference is still valid before calling `ResizeObserver.unobserve()`. When Blazor removes the DOM element before disposing the component, the lookup returns `undefined`, causing the TypeError. Additionally, since `SKCanvasView.Dispose()` calls `sizeWatcher?.Dispose()`, `interop?.Dispose()`, and `FreeBitmap()` sequentially without individual try-catch blocks, the exception also prevents proper cleanup of the pinned pixel buffer.

We don't have a clean user-side workaround for this yet. Catching the exception at the caller level would suppress the crash but would also skip the internal `FreeBitmap()` cleanup, leaking a pinned GCHandle. The fix needs to be in SkiaSharp itself — both a null guard in the JavaScript and defensive exception handling in the C# disposal chain.

Regarding `Dispose(bool)` — you're right that the current pattern doesn't allow derived classes to customize disposal. We'll address that as part of the fix.
